race.name: Engadin Skimarathon
race.tagline: Thirteen thousand skaters gliding across frozen Engadin lakes at 1,800 meters — the world's fastest marathon on the world's most beautiful course.

vitals.registration: Opens in spring; sells out within days
vitals.aid_stations: Multiple stations with Bündner Nusstorte, warm drinks, energy food

climate.description: At 1,800m altitude, conditions are reliably cold and sunny. The Engadin is famous for over 300 days of sunshine per year. Temperatures typically -10°C to 0°C on race day. Altitude ensures snow reliability but can cause respiratory stress for unacclimatized racers.
challenges: High altitude (1,800m) affects aerobic performance for unacclimatized skiers
Intense UV reflection off frozen lakes requires eye protection
Dry high-altitude air demands extra hydration
Occasional Maloja wind funnels through the pass

course.primary: Point-to-point through the Upper Engadin valley, crossing frozen lakes between Maloja and S-chanf. The course is fast and relatively flat, making it one of the fastest marathon courses in the world. Stunning views of the Bernina massif throughout.
course.surface: Machine-groomed skating lanes on frozen lake surfaces and valley floor
course.grooming: World-class Worldloppet standard on frozen lake surfaces
features: Skating across frozen Engadin lakes — unique in world skiing
Bernina massif panorama (4,049m) as constant backdrop
One of the fastest marathon courses due to flat lake crossings
Passes through St. Moritz — the birthplace of winter tourism
Feed stations serve Engadin Nusstorte (local nut cake)

nordic_lab_rating.score_note: Maximum altitude and scenery scores. The frozen lake crossings create a skiing experience available nowhere else. Shorter distance and flat profile keep technicality and elevation scores moderate.

history.summary: Founded in 1969 with 945 participants, the Engadin Skimarathon has grown to become the largest cross-country skiing event in Switzerland and one of the most iconic freestyle races in the world. The course crosses the frozen Engadin lakes at 1,800m altitude with views of the Bernina massif. The race helped establish the Upper Engadin as a premier cross-country skiing destination alongside its reputation for alpine skiing in St. Moritz.
notable_facts: First edition in 1969 drew 945 participants — now exceeds 13,500
Crosses frozen lakes including Lake Silvaplana and Lake St. Moritz
One of the founding members of the Worldloppet series
St. Moritz hosted the Winter Olympics in 1928 and 1948
The Engadin valley averages over 300 days of sunshine per year
Known as the fastest marathon course due to flat lake crossings and altitude-thinned air
